title: Qualified trait access: parser
analysis: This task adds two new expression forms to the parser: (1) `TypeName::ident` as QualifiedAccess (precedence 2, left-associative) and (2) `expr.(TypeName::ident)` as InstanceQualifiedAccess (precedence 8, left-associative postfix). The implementation spans three layers: grammar rules in tree-sitter-reify/grammar.js, AST node variants in reify-syntax/src/lib.rs (ExprKind enum), and CST→AST lowering functions in reify-syntax/src/ts_parser.rs. Both forms already exist in the current branch — grammar rules (qualified_access at prec.left(2), instance_qualified_access at prec.left(8)), AST variants (QualifiedAccess, InstanceQualifiedAccess), lowering functions (lower_qualified_access, lower_instance_qualified_access), and 7 integration tests all pass. The compiler crate already has a placeholder match arm for both variants that emits a diagnostic (resolution is out of scope for this parser task). Verification should confirm all 7 tests pass and no regressions exist.

prerequisites.description: Ensure tree-sitter-reify grammar includes qualified_access rule: `prec.left(2, seq(field('qualifier', $._expression), '::', field('member', $.identifier)))` and instance_qualified_access rule: `prec.left(8, seq(field('object', $._expression), '.', '(', field('qualified', $.qualified_access), ')'))`. Both must be listed in the _expression choice. Regenerate parser.c if grammar.js was modified.

steps.description: Write test `parse_basic_qualified_access`: parse `structure S { let x = Rigid::mass }`, assert no errors, assert the let value is ExprKind::QualifiedAccess with qualifier=Ident("Rigid") and member="mass".

Add ExprKind::QualifiedAccess { qualifier: Box<Expr>, member: String } variant to lib.rs. Add `lower_qualified_access` function in ts_parser.rs that reads 'qualifier' and 'member' fields from the tree-sitter node. Add dispatch case `"qualified_access" => self.lower_qualified_access(node)` in lower_expr. Verify step-1 test passes.

Write test `parse_chained_qualified_access`: parse `structure S { let x = A::B::c }`, assert nested QualifiedAccess { qualifier: QualifiedAccess { qualifier: Ident("A"), member: "B" }, member: "c" } — verifying left-associative chaining.

Verify left-associativity — the `prec.left(2, ...)` grammar rule handles chaining automatically. The recursive `lower_expr` call on the qualifier field naturally produces nested QualifiedAccess nodes. Step-3 test should pass without code changes.

Write test `parse_qualified_access_in_binary_expr`: parse `structure S { let x = Rigid::mass + 1 }`, assert BinOp { op: "+", left: QualifiedAccess(Rigid, mass), right: NumberLiteral(1.0) } — verifying precedence 2 binds tighter than precedence 5 additive but can participate in larger expressions.

Verify precedence interaction — QualifiedAccess at prec 2 is lower than additive prec 5, so `Rigid::mass + 1` parses as `(Rigid::mass) + 1` because `::` binds its operands before `+` claims the left side. Step-5 test should pass without code changes.

Write test `parse_basic_instance_qualified_access`: parse `structure S { let x = motor.(Driveable::torque) }`, assert InstanceQualifiedAccess { object: Ident("motor"), qualified: QualifiedAccess(Ident("Driveable"), "torque") }.

Add ExprKind::InstanceQualifiedAccess { object: Box<Expr>, qualified: Box<Expr> } variant to lib.rs. Add `lower_instance_qualified_access` function in ts_parser.rs that reads 'object' and 'qualified' fields. Add dispatch case `"instance_qualified_access" => self.lower_instance_qualified_access(node)` in lower_expr. Verify step-7 test passes.

Write test `parse_instance_qualified_with_member_chain`: parse `structure S { let x = self.motor.(Driveable::torque) }`, assert InstanceQualifiedAccess { object: MemberAccess(Ident("self"), "motor"), qualified: QualifiedAccess(Ident("Driveable"), "torque") } — verifying that the object field can be a chained member access expression.

Verify member chain interaction — both instance_qualified_access and member_access are at prec.left(8), so `self.motor.(...)` parses left-to-right: first `self.motor` as MemberAccess, then `(MemberAccess).(...)` as InstanceQualifiedAccess. Step-9 test should pass without code changes.

Write test `parse_instance_qualified_with_chained_path`: parse `structure S { let x = obj.(A::B::c) }`, assert InstanceQualifiedAccess { object: Ident("obj"), qualified: QualifiedAccess { qualifier: QualifiedAccess(Ident("A"), "B"), member: "c" } } — verifying the qualified field supports chained qualified paths.
Verify chained path inside instance qualified — the grammar constrains `field('qualified', $.qualified_access)` which is itself left-recursive. Step-11 test should pass without code changes.

Write test `parse_distinct_ast_nodes`: parse `structure S { let a = Rigid::mass  let b = obj.(Rigid::mass) }`, assert first member is QualifiedAccess and second is InstanceQualifiedAccess — verifying both forms produce distinct AST node variants as required by the task.

design_decisions.decision: QualifiedAccess qualifier is Box<Expr> (not String) to support chaining
design_decisions.rationale: Chained qualified access like `A::B::c` requires the qualifier to be an expression that can itself be a QualifiedAccess. Using Box<Expr> enables left-recursive chaining: `(A::B)::c` where `A::B` is a QualifiedAccess and the outer `(A::B)::c` is also a QualifiedAccess. A String qualifier would only allow single-level `TypeName::ident`.

design_decisions.decision: Qualified access at precedence 2 (same level as &&)
design_decisions.rationale: Precedence 2 means `::` binds tighter than logical operators (&&, ||) but looser than comparison, arithmetic, and postfix operators. This allows `Rigid::mass + 1` to parse as `(Rigid::mass) + 1` naturally, and `A::b && C::d` to parse as `(A::b) && (C::d)`. This matches the intuition that `::` is a tight access operator, though not as tight as `.` (prec 8).

design_decisions.decision: Instance qualified access constrains inner field to $.qualified_access (not $._expression)
design_decisions.rationale: The grammar rule `field('qualified', $.qualified_access)` prevents nonsensical forms like `expr.(42)` or `expr.(x + y)`. Only `TypeName::ident` chains are allowed inside the parenthesized part. This is enforced at the grammar level for free.

design_decisions.decision: Instance qualified access at precedence 8 (postfix level)
design_decisions.rationale: Instance qualified access `expr.(T::m)` is syntactically a postfix operator on the object expression, like member access `expr.member`. Putting it at prec 8 (same as member_access, function_call, index_access) allows natural left-to-right chaining: `self.motor.(Driveable::torque)` parses as `(self.motor).(Driveable::torque)`.

design_decisions.decision: No separate TypeName AST node — qualifier uses Ident
design_decisions.rationale: At the parser level, `Rigid` in `Rigid::mass` is just an identifier. Semantic analysis (trait resolution in the compiler) will distinguish type names from variable names. Keeping the qualifier as a plain Expr avoids premature semantic coupling in the parser.

reuse.how: Add two new match arms (`"qualified_access"` and `"instance_qualified_access"`) following the same pattern as other expression kinds like `"member_access"` and `"binary_expression"`.

reuse.what: lower_member_access structure as template for lower_qualified_access
reuse.how: Both functions read a node and two named fields, lower one as expression and one as text, then construct an ExprKind variant. The qualified_access lowering mirrors this pattern exactly.
Add two new variants following the established pattern of doc comments + field types. QualifiedAccess mirrors MemberAccess structure; InstanceQualifiedAccess is similar but with two Box<Expr> fields.
Reuse the standard test pattern: `parse_decls(source) -> (Vec<Declaration>, Vec<ParseError>)` calling `reify_syntax::parse()` with a test ModulePath. This pattern is used across all 20 test files.
The qualified_access rule uses `prec.left(N, seq(field(...), token, field(...)))` identical to how binary_expression and member_access define left-associative rules.
